scope.summary: bead_tracker_guard treated every non-parent-child dependency as a blocker, so a discovered-from provenance edge refused the close; the predicate is now an allowlist of non-sequencing edge types.
out_of_scope: `related` and any other br dependency type: deliberately still blocking. The allowlist is exactly the two edges proven non-sequencing (parent-child, discovered-from); anything else refuses, so a future br type cannot silently widen the exemption.
br's own dependency check, which is unchanged. The guard still passes --force for a non-sequencing edge because br does not share the distinction.
The beads already converted to parent-child by hand on 2026-07-21; they remain correct and were not rewritten back.

entry_points.name: scripts/bead_tracker_guard.sh --selftest
entry_points.trace: 10 assertions pass, 4 of them new: an open discovered-from edge yields force-non-sequencing instead of refusing (the reported bug); a provenance edge never excuses a sibling `blocks` edge; an unknown type (sequenced-after) still refuses, proving the exemption is an allowlist; and a closed dependency needs no force at all. The pre-existing parent-child and real-blocker assertions still hold.

entry_points.name: scripts/bead_tracker_guard.sh close
entry_points.trace: This bead was closed through the fixed guard itself at the commit recorded in close_reason, so the close path was exercised end to end rather than only unit-tested.

limitations: Discriminating, verified by mutation: removing discovered-from from the allowlist fails the new assertion with 'refusing open blocking dependencies: epic(discovered-from:in_progress)'.

No live bead currently carries an OPEN discovered-from dependency — the one the report named (oraclemcp-yi5bu) is already closed and its parent bp8ia.3 has since closed — so the end-to-end proof is the selftest plus this bead's own guarded close, not a replay of the original refusal.
shellcheck -S warning is clean; there is no Rust surface in this change.
